### Endogenous Timing {#sec-endogenous-timing}

#### Management's Incentive

::: {#prp-mgmt}
#### Management's Timing Preference

Management prefers moving first (Subgame ML) to moving second
(Subgame LM) if and only if

$$
V^3\kappa^2 \geq 4V^2 - 4V\mu + \mu^2 + \delta^2.
$$ {#eq-mgmt-cond}
:::

::: {.proof}
This condition is exactly

$$
\E\!\left[U_M^{ML}\right] \geq \E\!\left[U_M^{S}\right],
$$

using @eq-eum-sim and @eq-eum-lm.
:::

#### Implications for Labor

The previous section implies a clean partition of Labor's timing
incentives.

::: {#cor-labor-regions}
#### Timing Regions for the Informed Player

For the informed player, the timing logic is:

1. both types prefer moving first when $R_\ell \leq 0$;
2. only the low type prefers moving second when $R_\ell > 0 \geq R_H$;
3. both types prefer moving second when $R_H > 0$.
:::

::: {#rem-fu-extension}
#### Mean and Range

Relative to \citet{fu2006}, the binary-distribution extension isolates two
new mechanisms. First, the mean $\mu$ shifts both type values and the
belief term $\kappa$. Second, the spread $\delta$ changes the gap
between $u_\ell$ and $u_H$ and therefore the overlap between their
first-mover intervals. Timing incentives are therefore jointly governed
by the mean and the range, not by the mean comparison alone.
:::

::: {#rem-no-pooling-ambiguity}
#### No Pooling Ambiguity

Because the LM subgame is uniquely separating, timing choices in the
endogenous game can be type-contingent without any pooling ambiguity.
When @eq-mgmt-cond also holds, Management is willing to commit early;
when it fails, Management prefers to wait for Labor's revealed type.
:::
